critic_raw:
This crop yield prediction experiment uses a CNN on MODIS satellite spectral data compared against a linear regression baseline on historical agricultural data across five growing seasons and multiple regions. Strengths include preprocessing fit on training data only, MAE as primary metric, validation set for hyperparameter tuning, and test set isolation.

The stratified random split across regions and crop types is the main structural concern. Satellite imagery-derived crop yield has strong spatial autocorrelation (adjacent fields share climate and soil characteristics) and temporal autocorrelation (consecutive growing seasons share weather patterns). A random split that assigns some fields from a region to training and others to test ignores that nearby fields share environmental context. Proper holdout requires geographically disjoint regions, not a random sample of all region-crop combinations.

The baseline comparison is materially unfair: the CNN uses spectral indices from MODIS augmented with climatic data, while the linear regression uses 'historical agricultural data.' If historical yield data contains information not available to the CNN (e.g., farmer-reported soil conditions, local crop variety), the baselines are not operating on equivalent information sets. Both models should receive the same input feature set to isolate the architecture contribution.

Domain knowledge suggests temporal autocorrelation in crop yield is strong — El Niño/La Niña cycles, drought years, and regional agricultural policy changes create multi-year yield patterns. The claim that 'temporal dependencies are minimized by balancing data across the cross-section' does not adequately address inter-annual climate cycles. A held-out final growing season as the test set would be a far stronger validation of generalizability.
all_issues_raised: Spatial autocorrelation not addressed: adjacent fields share climate and soil context; random region-level split inflates generalization estimates
Baseline information asymmetry: CNN uses spectral + climatic data while linear regression uses historical agricultural data — models do not receive equivalent inputs
Temporal autocorrelation from inter-annual climate cycles (El Niño, drought years) not addressed by cross-sectional balancing approach
PCA preprocessing applied to spectral data without specifying components retained — spatial resolution of MODIS makes PCA sensitivity significant
No holdout growing season as temporal test — using a random subset of all five seasons does not test year-out-of-sample generalization

Spatial autocorrelation leakage from random regional split: geographically adjacent fields share soil, topography, and microclimate characteristics; random assignment of field-regions to train and test inflates generalization estimates relative to spatially blocked holdout

Temporal structure of growing seasons discarded: random split mixes seasons into train and test rather than evaluating generalization to a future season; chronological holdout better reflects deployment reality for crop yield forecasting

Feature-asymmetric baseline comparison: the CNN receives MODIS spectral indices while the linear regression baseline uses historical agricultural data, confounding architecture comparison with input data richness

PCA applied before CNN input is architecturally counterproductive: CNNs are designed to learn spatial features end-to-end; pre-processing with PCA reduces the spatial structure the CNN could exploit, potentially underestimating CNN capability

Temporal alignment between satellite overpass and yield measurement not addressed: spectral indices are computed from fixed overpass times that may not align with the optimal phenological window for yield prediction
Spatial autocorrelation unaddressed: stratified random split across regions still leaks spatially adjacent fields within regions into train and test
PCA applied before CNN destroys spatial structure that convolutional layers are designed to exploit — fundamentally undermines CNN's architectural advantage
Data modality confound: CNN uses spectral imagery while baseline uses historical agricultural data — different inputs, not just different models
Five-season window may span agricultural regime changes (crop rotation, irrigation adoption) not addressed in the temporal design
MODIS spatial resolution (250m-1km) may be too coarse for field-level yield prediction in fragmented agricultural landscapes
